coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on quenched carbon dots-rhodamine B for selective detection of l-cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Ya-Chuan
Zhao, Qi
Wu, Xue-Ying
Li, Zhen
Lu, Ying-Jie
Liu, Qian
Dong, Lin
Shan, Chong-Xin
coredata.dc:description: The level of l-Cysteine (Cys) is an indicator for many diseases of human, thus the selective and sensitive detection of abnormal level of Cys is critically important for human health. In this paper, quenched-carbon dots- Rhodamine B (QCR) system has been developed for the selective and sensitive detection of Cys, where Hg2+-quenched carbon dots (CDs) serve as the reporting unit and the Rhodamine B as the reference unit. The detection limit for the Cys is 52.10 nM, which are much lower than the normal concentration in human urine. Moreover, the sensor shows excellent selectivity for Cys over other amino acids and common cellular ions and environmentally relevant anions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe
l-cysteine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of as-prepared CDs and the inset shows the high resolution TEM image of the as-prepared CDs. (b) The XRD pattern of the CDs. (c) The absorption, excitation and fluorescence spectra of CDs. The inset shows the corresponding images of the CDs under indoor and UV lighting conditions. (d) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs under different excitation wavelength.
(a) XPS survey spectra, and (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s high resolution spectra of the CDs.

(a) (b) The fluorescence spectra of rhodamine B and CDs under different concentration of Hg2+. (c) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs, CDs + Hg2++Cys, CDs + Hg2+. (d) The absorption spectra of CDs, rhodamine B and CDs + rhodamine B.

(a) Transient photoluminescence spectra of CDs, CDs + Hg2++Cys, CDs + Hg2+. (b) Absorption spectrum of CDs, CDs + Hg2++Cys, CDs + Hg2+. (c) Schematic illustration of the quenching and recovery mechanism of sensor.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the QCR sensor dependent on the concentration of Cys and the inset shows the corresponding images of QCR sensor in the absence and presence of Cys under UV illumination. (b) The dependence of fluorescence intensity on the concentration of Cys and the inset shows the linear relationship between the Cys and fluorescence intensity ratio (I 437/I 586) with the concentration of Cys in the investigated range from 6 μM to 16 μM.
(a) The selectivity of sensor to Cys in the presence of other interference.
The cell viability of the CDs-Hg2+-rhodamine B system estimated by MTT assay.
The ratiometric fluorescent sensor of Cys based on dual emissive quenched-carbon dots-rhodamine B.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on quenched carbon dots-rhodamine B for selective detection of l-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, School of Physics and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The level of l-Cysteine (Cys) is an indicator for many diseases of human, thus the selective and sensitive detection of abnormal level of Cys is critically important for human health. In this paper, quenched-carbon dots- Rhodamine B (QCR) system has been developed for the selective and sensitive detection of Cys, where Hg2+-quenched carbon dots (CDs) serve as the reporting unit and the Rhodamine B as the reference unit. The detection limit for the Cys is 52.10 nM, which are much lower than the normal concentration in human urine. Moreover, the sensor shows excellent selectivity for Cys over other amino acids and common cellular ions and environmentally relevant anions.

ce:section.ce:para: Early detection of abnormal human body index is an important requirement for the definite diagnosis and treatment of diseases. As one of the essential amino acids in human body, l-Cysteine (Cys) plays a critical role on cardiovascular system and metabolic system connected with various diseases, such as coronary heart disease and Parkinson's disease [1–4]. Thus the selective and sensitive detection of Cys is of great importance and significance in monitoring the health of humans.

Currently, approaches for the detection of Cys include atomic absorption spectrometry [5], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [6], inductive coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy [7], mass spectrometry, gas chromatography, and capillary electrophoresis [8]. However, most of these methods involve expensive instrumentation and complex operations, which will increase the detection costs and environmental risks. To address the above problems, fluorescent sensor method is an ideal candidate which shows a low detection limit for rapid, sensitive, and selective response to analyte [1,4,9,10]. In particular, nanomaterials are considered to be competitive candidates as fluorescent sensor because of their advantages of small size, controlled fluorescence, surface activity, etc. To date, various fluorescent nanomaterials have been investigated as sensor for detecting Cys, such as rare earth hybrid material [11], noble metal nanoparticles [12] and semiconductor quantum dots [13]. Wang et al. developed a fluorescent nanoprobe based on luminescent lanthanide graphene hybrid material for turn-on sensing of l-Cysteine [14]. However, rare earths are non-renewable resources, and the mining and purifying of them often involves complex operations. Bhamore et al. reported an l-Cysteine sensor based on functionalized gold nanoclusters and gold nanoparticles [12]. But expensive precursor and complex synthesis routes are usually needed in preparing gold nanoparticles, which increases detection cost and hinders their widespread applications. Liu et al. developed a mercaptoacetic acid-capped CdSe/ZnS quantum dots in order to detect l-Cysteine [15]. It is worth special attention that Cd ions, as heavy metal, are leading to toxicity concerns. Therefore, it is still urgently needed to develop a facile and effective strategy to detect l-Cysteine on basis of fluorescent sensor method.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) has attracted increasing research interest in the field of biosensing [16–18], drug delivery [19–21], labelling [22–24], and photoelectric devices [25–28] due to their unique properties, such as superior photo-stability, robust chemical inertness, good water solubility and favourable biocompatibility [18,29–31]. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots (CdTe, CdSe), CDs with higher photo-stability and eco-friendly character have been used as a probe in the field of bio-applications [32–34]. Lan et al. reported an l-Cysteine sensor based on carbon nanoparticle-based fluorescent probe [17]. However, the concentration of the analyte is usually measured by sole emission peak and the color remains the same. In this case, it is difficult to judge whether fluorescent intensity enhancement or decrease is due to the analyte-induced change or operation errors, or instrumental efficiency or environmental factors, especially at very low analyte concentration. Alternatively, dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform is believed to conquer these problems, where one emission peak acts as a reference while the other as the receptor and the response signal to recognize the analyte. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor can eliminate background interferences and environmental effect due to built-in correction of the ratiometric design, which can significantly improve the sensitivity and accuracy of the sensors. Therefore, it is interesting and meaningful to develop ratiometric fluorescent sensors for the detection of l-Cysteine, thereby making a great contribution to disease diagnosis. Nevertheless, none such report can be found to date.

In this paper, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) with strong blue fluorescence was prepared by a hydrothermal process using trisodium citrate dihydrate and melamine as precursors. The as-prepared CDs are endowed with abundant functional groups, strong luminescence, and good water stability, which facilitate the proposed strategies using CDs as the reporter signal in dual-emission fluorescent sensors. Subsequently, quenched-carbon dots-Rhodamine B (QCR) systems were constructed as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of l-Cysteine. As shown in Scheme 1 , this sensor consists of two emission peaked at 437 nm and 586 nm, which can be assigned to the emission of CDs and Rhodamine B, respectively. The Hg2+-quenched CDs acts as the reporting unit while the Rhodamine B acts as the reference unit. This system shows high selectivity toward l-Cysteine over other interfering ions and amino acids.

Water-soluble The CDs used in this work were prepared by a hydrothermal method employing melamine and trisodium citrate dehydrate as the reaction precursors. In order to investigate the morphology of the CDs, TEM measurement was carried out. Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of the CDs, revealing that the nanoparticles have a spherical-like shape. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the CDs was displayed in the inset of Fig. 1a. Interplanar spacing with a distance of around 0.34 nm can be observed, corresponding to a graphitic structure. The XRD pattern of the CDs is shown in Fig. 1b. The broad peak at around 24° confirms the graphite structure of the CDs.

The optical properties of the CDs were investigated by using a UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The CDs show two absorption bands centered at around 240 and 350 nm, as shown in Fig. 1c. The former can be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC/CN, while the latter to the n-p* transition of CO/CNH2 bond. The CDs aqueous solution shows a strong blue fluorescence under UV illumination (inset of Fig. 1c), while they are transparent under sunlight. Under the excitation of 350 nm, the CDs show a strong blue emission peak centered at 437 nm (Fig. 1c). It can be clearly seen that the intensity of the emission peak remains essentially unchanged with the increase of excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 410 nm (Fig. 1d), and the maximum emission intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. It is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs can be maintained even dispersing in high ionic strength aqueous solution (Fig. S1, supporting information). Moreover, photostability is a key issue to determine the performance of fluorescent sensor. Attractively, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs can maintain constant even after irradiation of 365 nm for 3600 s (Fig. S2, Supporting information). Importantly, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is highly stable in a wide pH range from 4 to 12, as shown in Fig. S3 (Supporting information), indicating the good stability of the synthesized CDs.

The surface state of the CDs was characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), as shown in Fig. S4 (Supporting information). The peaks at 3035 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH, and the peaks at 1558 cm−1 can be assigned to the NH stretching vibration mode, which indicates the existence of NH3. The above results indicate that the CDs were surrounded by NH3 and OH groups. To shed more light on the surface composition of the CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed. The XPS survey spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2 ) presents three peaks at 284.5, 399.8 and 531.5 eV, which can be assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Moreover, the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) can be deconvoluted into three typical peaks centered at 284.7, 286.1 and 288.1 eV, which correspond to CC/CC, CN/CO and CN/CO, respectively. Besides, the fitting of the N1s spectrum shows two peaks at 399.27 and 400.32 eV, respectively, revealing two different types of nitrides: pyridinic type and NH (Fig. 2c). The O 1s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks at 531.0, 532.08 and 535.5 eV (Fig. 2d), corresponding to CO, C O C/COH and COOH, respectively, which is consistent with the characteristic carbon state of CO in the C 1s spectrum. The above results are in accordance with the FTIR spectra (Fig. S4, Supporting information). The FTIR and XPS characterizations indicated that the surface of the CDs were functionalized with amino, hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl groups. These polar functional groups endowed the CDs with a good water stability and strong metal-ligand affinity between the CDs and Hg2+.

In order to obtain a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform, rhodamine B was selected as the reference unit serving as a built-in calibration to eliminate background interferences and environmental effects for the following reasons: Firstly, there are no overlap between the fluorescence spectrum of CDs and rhodamine B, which is necessary for accurate ratiometric detection, especially at low analyte concentration. Secondly, the fluorescence of the CDs and rhodamine B can be excited under the same excitation source (Fig. S5, Supporting information). Thirdly, the fluorescence intensity of the rhodamine B can maintain constant even in the presence of Hg2+ up to 6 μM (Fig. 3 a) due to its chemical inertness. Moreover, the fluorescence of CDs centered at 437 nm dramatically decreases with the concentration of Hg2+ increasing from 0 μM to 6 μM and will be completely quenched at Hg2+ concentration of 6 μM (Fig. 3b). It is worth noting that the fluorescence of the quenched CDs can be obviously recovered when l-Cysteine was added in the CDs-Hg2+ system (Fig. 3c). Interestingly, this phenomenon can be directly observed by naked eye under UV irradiation. Therefore, the CDs-Hg2+ quenching system was selected as the report unit.

The interaction between CDs and rhodamine B in the hybrid system is a crucial factor that determines the performance of this ratiometric fluorescent sensor, thus the interaction between CDs and rhodamine B is accessed by UV–Vis absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 3d, the CDs show an absorption peak at 335 nm, and there is an observable absorption band of rhodamine B from 325 nm to 375 nm. The overlap of absorption band suggests that the dual emissive ratiometric fluorescent sensor can be achieved under a single excitation source. The fluorescent spectra of the as-fabricated CDs-rhodamine B hybrid system consist of two emission peaks centered at around 437 nm and 586 nm under the excitation of 350 nm, which can be attributed to the emission from the CDs and rhodamine B, respectively, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra is shown in Fig. S5 (Supporting information). The above results demonstrate that quenched-CDs-rhodamine B (QCR) system has a great potential for designing ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of l-Cysteine in aqueous media.

To understand the structure of this nano-sensor, Zeta potential, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), and UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured. Firstly, the Zeta potential of carbon dots and Rhodamine B aqueous solution are measured to be +38.9 and −11.53 mV, respectively, as shown in Figs. S6(a and b). It means that carbon dots possess positively charged surface and negatively for Rhodamine B, which shaped situation for the electrostatic attraction between them. The Zeta potential of the mixed aqueous solution Fig. S6(c) is +12.54 mV, indicating Rhodamine B has adsorbed on CDs. Secondly, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was measured (Fig. S7a) to investigate the variation of surface functional groups. The characteristic vibration centered at 1558 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 3035 cm−1 come from CDs and the characteristic vibration of Rhodamine B is 2024 cm−1 and 2171 cm−1, respectively. For the mixed specimen, only peaks from CDs and RhB (1558 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, 3035 cm−1, 2024 cm−1 and 2171 cm−1) are detected. Since no other peaks are observed, It can be speculated that Rhodamine B has not bonded with CDs covalently. Additionally, this hypothesis was further confirmed by UV–Vis absorption spectra Fig. S7(b). The mixed aqueous show an absorption peak at 335 nm from CDs and 350 and 550 nm of Rhodamine B, respectively. There are no new peaks, suggesting CDs and Rhodamine B are attached together as they are originally.

In order to understand the fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism of the CDs, the transient photoluminescence spectrum was performed by monitoring the emission at 437 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs, CDs + Hg2+, CDs + Hg2++Cys can be well fitted using the following exponential decay formula:
(1)
y =
y
0
+
A
1
exp
( − x / τ )
where y is the emission intensity, y 0, A 1 are constant, τ is lifetime of the emission. The lifetimes of CDs, CDs + Hg2+, CDs + Hg2++Cys are calculated to be 29.25 ns, 4.66 ns, 29.21 ns, respectively. The decay time of the CDs in the presence of Hg2+ is distinctively shorter than that for the CDs. It is speculated that some generated electrons have been transferred from the CDs to the Hg2+, leading to the shortened lifetime. The possible reason for lifetime recovery after adding Cys is that Hg2+ can be removed from the surface of the CDs because of the higher affinity between Hg2+ and Cys, which interrupts the charge transfer between the CDs and Hg2+ thus leads to fluorescence recovery.

To further understand the sensing mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent sensor, UV–vis absorption measurement was conducted. The UV–vis absorption spectra shown in Fig. 4b reveal that the absorption peak of CDs blueshifts from 346 nm it to 337 nm with the introduction of Hg2+, while the absorption peak of CDs will almost redshift to the original values in the presence of Cys. This suggests that the Hg2+ can influence the surface state of CDs and result in a charge transfer process. The Hg2+ could be removed from the surface of CDs by forming Hg-Cys chelation. On the basis of the above analysis, the detection mechanism illustration has been given in Fig. 4c.

The response time is critical parameter for detection of Cys. The time-dependent fluorescence response of the QCR to Cys is monitored at 437 nm with excitation at 350 nm (Fig. S8, Supporting information). The curve shows that the fluorescence intensity increases to the maximum value in 120 s and stays at the maximum value after 120 s, enabling rapid detection of Cys.

Since Cys can chelate with Hg2+ to form a stable complex, the mixture solution of quenched CDs-Hg2+ and rhodamine B constitute a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting Cys. The ratiometric fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs (emission peak at 437 nm, I 437) and rhodamine B (emission peak at 586 nm, I 586) was used for quantitative determination to eliminate external interference and improve the accuracy of the fluorescence sensor. To verify the performance of QCR-based sensor, the sensitivity of the Cys detection was investigated firstly. The concentration of Cys can be quantitatively evaluated by analyzing the dependence of the fluorescent intensity ratio (I 437/I 586). As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 437/I 586) increases gradually with the concentration of Cys increasing from 0 μM to 25 μM until it reaches saturation when the concentration of Cys is higher than 20 μM. The fluorescence intensity ratio versus the concentration of Cys was shown in Fig. 5b. Moreover, a good linear relationship between the Cys and fluorescence intensity ratio (I 437/I 586) was obtained with the concentration of Cys in the investigated range from 6 μM to 16 μM (R2 = 0.9866). The detection limit of the nanoprobe has been determined to be 52.10 nM using three times the standard deviation of background (3Sb/m) suggested by IUPAC, where Sb is the standard deviation of the blank (n = 20) and m is the slope of the linearity. The above result was comparable to that of the previously reported CDs-based fluorescent sensors. The corresponding calculation process was shown in Table S1 (Supporting information).

Three reasons are responsible for the highly sensitive detection of Cys. Firstly, Cys can chelate with Hg2+ to form a stable complex. Upon chelation with Hg2+, the fluorescence of the CDs can be restored. Secondly, in the QCR, the emission of CDs at 437 nm act as a receptor while the emission of rhodamine B at 586 nm act as a reference. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor can eliminate background interferences and environmental effects due to built-in correction of the ratiometric design, which can significantly improve the sensitivity and accuracy of the sensors especially at very low analyte concentrations. Thirdly, the good water solubility and the rich surface functional groups of the CDs provide abundant active sites for the analyte, contributing to a high sensitivity.

Selectivity is another important parameter for fluorescent probe. The selectivity of the QCR sensor for Cys towards various potentially interference has been evaluated, including representative amino acids and common cellular ions such as tyrosine (Tyr), aspartic acid (Asp), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), glutamic acid and l-phenylalanine, as well as Na+, K+, Ca2+, each at 20 μM, as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the QCR sensor does not show obvious change with the introduction of various interfering amino acid or common cellular ions. Moreover, the response of QCR sensor to Cys was not interfered by all of interferences. Only Cys exhibits a noticeable response, indicating that QCR sensor has good selectivity towards Cys.

Overall, the above results clearly demonstrate that CDs-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor have excellent selectivity and anti-external interference ability for effective recognition of the Cys.

To verify the feasibility of QCR sensor for biological applications, MTT assays were performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the sensor to the MDCK cells. After incubating 24 h, the cell viability of the MDCK cells can maintain than 80%, even upon addition of CDs up to 400 μg/mL (Fig. 7 ). There is also negligible cytotoxicity to the MDCK cells when rhodamine B was introduced in detection system. As a result, the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor proposed in this paper has a negligible biological toxicity and it can be used in biological applications.

In summary, fluorescent CDs were prepared through a hydrothermal approach and a QCR sensor for the sensitive and selective detection of Cys has been obtained by employing CDs as the reporting unit and rhodamine B as the internal reference. The Hg2+ ions in the surface of CDs can be chelated with Cys to form a stable complex, leading fluorescence recovery of CDs. The fluorescent emission of Hg2+-quenched CDs can be recovered upon addition of Cys due to chelation mechanism. The QCR sensor showed a good linear detection of Cys in the range of 6 μM–16 μM (R2 = 0.9866) with a detection limit of 52.10 nM. The results reported in this paper may provide a route for the sensitive and selective detection of Cys in the future clinical analysis.

The precursors used in this study include melamine (C3H6N6, purity>99%), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, purity>99%), NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, NaCl, Phenylalanine (Phe), glutamic acid (Glu), lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), aspartic acid (Asp), tyrosine (Tyr), etc. All of the chemicals were purchased from Macklin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Note that all the chemicals used in this work were analytical grade without further purification.

The CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method employing sodium citrate as carbon source and melamine as nitrogen source. The typical preparation process is as follows: sodium citrate (1.74 mmol) and melamine (3.6 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL distilled water. Then the solution was transferred into a Teflonlined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, the CDs were centrifuged at 12 000 rpm for 20 min to remove large precipitates and the concentrated supernatant was purified by silica gel column chromatography. Finally, CDs aqueous solution was obtained.

20 μL of as-prepared CDs was dissolved in 2 mL distilled water then 6 μM of Hg2+ solution was added to above CDs aqueous solution in order to quench the fluorescence of CDs. In order to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor, 0.5 μM rhodamine B was introduced as a reference unit. The calculated amount of Cys was then added into above mixture. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted for three times.

MDCK cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (1 × 104 cell/well) in culture media under a 5% CO2/95% air incubator for 24 h. The culture media was aspirated and the cells were washed three times using PBS buffer solution. Then, the cells were incubated with fresh culture media containing CDs and Hg2+ or rhodamine B. MDCK cells were incubated for additional 24 h. Cell viability was measured by CCK-8 assay with 450 nm wavelength. The cell viability measurements were conducted for three times.

X-ray X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on X'Pert Pro diffractometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2010) was employed to characterize the size and crystallinity of the samples. Fluorescence spectra were recorded n Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. The UV–Vis absorption of the CDs was obtained using a UH4150 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer from dry KBr discs, over the range 4000-400 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the samples were collected by using a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer equipped with an Al Ka X-ray radiation source, and the XPS binding energy was internally referenced to the C 1s peak (BE = 284.8 eV).
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (51602288 and 21601159), the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (61425021).
